perturbation.original_text: Investor shall obligate its employees and the employees of its affiliates who shall have access to any portion of the Information to protect the confidential and proprietary nature of the Information.
perturbation.changed_text: Investor Signature Name Title Date Address where information should be sent: Investor shall obligate its employees and the employees of its affiliates who shall have access to any portion of the Information to protect the confidential and proprietary nature of the Information.
perturbation.explanation: Moving the disclosure about employee obligations to the end, after the signature block, buries it and makes it less prominent.
perturbation.law_explanation: NUTSA requires reasonable efforts to maintain the secrecy of trade secrets. Burying the employee obligation clause makes it less likely that employees will be properly informed and thus less likely that reasonable efforts are being made to protect the trade secret.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: NRS: CHAPTER 600A - TRADE SECRETS (UNIFORM ACT) [Rev. 6/29/2024 4:37:37 PM--2023] CHAPTER 600A - TRADE SECRETS (UNIFORM ACT) NRS 600A.010 Short title. NRS 600A.030 Definitions. NRS 600A.032 Presumption of reasonable effort by owner to maintain secrecy; rebuttal of presumption. NRS 600A.035 Theft of trade secrets prohibited; criminal penalties. NRS 600A.040 Actual or threatened misappropriation: Injunctive relief. NRS 600A.050 Misappropriation: Damages. NRS 600A.055 Misappropriation and posting or dissemination on Internet: Effect. NRS 600A.060 Attorneys fees. NRS 600A.070 Preservation of secrecy. NRS 600A.080 Limitation of actions. NRS 600A.090 Effect of chapter on other law and remedies. NRS 600A.100 Inapplicability of chapter to acts which occurred before, or began before and continue after, July 1, 1987. _________ NRS 600A.010 Short title. This chapter may be cited as the Uniform Trade Secrets Act. (Added to NRS by 1987, 19 ) NRS 600A.030 Definitions. As used in this chapter, unless the context otherwise requires: 1. Improper means includes, without limitation: (a) Theft; (b) Bribery; (c) Misrepresentation; (d) Willful breach or willful inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy; (e) Willful breach or willful inducement of a breach of a duty imposed by common law, statute, contract, license, protective order or other court or administrative order; and (f) Espionage through electronic or other means. 2. Misappropriation means: (a) Acquisition of the trade secret of another by a person by improper means; (b) Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (c) Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: (1) Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; (2) At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his or her knowledge of the trade secret was: (I) Derived from or through a person who had used improper means to acquire it; (II) Acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (III) Derived from or through a person who owed a duty to the person seeking relief to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (3) Before a material change of his or her position, knew or had reason
perturbation.justification: The modified text creates a structural flaw that leads to a legal contradiction under the NUTSA because it buries a critical disclosure regarding employee obligations to protect confidential information. The 'Perturbation Explanation' states that moving the employee obligation clause to the end, after the signature block, reduces its prominence. The 'Law Explanation' correctly states that this arrangement makes it less likely employees will be properly informed, undermining reasonable efforts to protect trade secrets as required by NUTSA. Scraped Law Snippet 1, specifically NRS 600A.030, defines 'Improper means' which includes 'Willful breach or willful inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy.' By burying the obligation, the contract increases the risk of such breaches, creating a structural flaw that makes it harder to enforce employee obligations, thereby contradicting the underlying principles of trade secret protection under NUTSA. The placement affects its clarity and enforceability, creating a structural flaw as defined in the instructions.

perturbation.original_text: Any obligation of investor as set forth in the preceding paragraph shall apply to information disclosed in writing and designated confidential, or if disclosed orally, shall be promptly reduced to writing.
perturbation.changed_text: Investor Signature Name Title Date Address where information should be sent: Any obligation of investor as set forth in the preceding paragraph shall apply to information disclosed in writing and designated confidential, or if disclosed orally, shall be promptly reduced to writing.
perturbation.explanation: Moving the disclosure about the form of confidential information to the end, after the signature block, buries it and makes it less prominent.
perturbation.law_explanation: NUTSA requires reasonable efforts to maintain the secrecy of trade secrets. Burying the disclosure about oral vs. written information makes it less likely to be adhered to. If oral disclosures are not reduced to writing promptly, they may not be protected by trade secret law.

perturbation.justification: The modification introduces a structural flaw that undermines the duty to maintain secrecy of trade secrets, creating a legal contradiction. By moving the disclosure requirement regarding written vs. oral communication to the end of the document, after the signature block, the modified text makes it less likely that the investor will adhere to the requirement of reducing oral disclosures to writing. Snippet 1 defines "improper means" as including "willful breach or willful inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy," and failing to properly disclose the requirements for maintaining secrecy could be seen as a breach of that duty. The original text places the obligation upfront, where it's more likely to be noticed and followed. The revised location undermines this obligation.

perturbation.original_text: Investor shall keep in confidence and not use the Information for its commercial benefit (except for technical and economic evaluation internal to investor). investor shall further keep in confidence and not disclose any part of the Information to a third party or parties or use the business plan without the written consent of TTI.
perturbation.changed_text: 2. Any obligation of investor as set forth in the preceding paragraph shall apply to information disclosed in writing and designated confidential, or if disclosed orally, shall be promptly reduced to writing. Investor shall keep in confidence and not use the Information for its commercial benefit (except for technical and economic evaluation internal to investor). investor shall further keep in confidence and not disclose any part of the Information to a third party or parties or use the business plan without the written consent of TTI. 3. Investor shall obligate its employees and the employees of its affiliates who shall have access to any portion of the Information to protect the confidential and proprietary nature of the Information. Investor Signature Name Title Date Address where information should be sent:
perturbation.explanation: Moving the core confidentiality obligation into the definition of what is covered by the obligation weakens its enforceability.
perturbation.law_explanation: NUTSA requires clear and unambiguous agreements to protect trade secrets. By obscuring the actual obligation with definitional text, it makes it harder to prove that the investor understood and agreed to the core confidentiality requirement.

perturbation.justification: The modified text creates a potential legal contradiction under Nevada's Uniform Trade Secrets Act (NUTSA). By moving the core confidentiality obligation into the definitional section and adding conditions (writing requirement), the enforceability is weakened. According to the Law Explanation, this obfuscation makes it harder to prove understanding and agreement, which is necessary for trade secret protection. Scraped Law Snippet 1, NRS 600A.030, defines 'Improper means' to include 'willful breach or willful inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy' and 'Misappropriation' as the 'Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent'. If the confidentiality obligation is weakened by its placement and conditions, it could be more difficult to prove a 'willful breach' or unauthorized 'disclosure or use', thus undermining the protection afforded by NUTSA. The original text provided a more direct and easily enforceable confidentiality obligation, while the changed text introduces ambiguity and potential loopholes.
